primary_diagnosis.name: Acute fasting hypoglycaemia with hypoglycaemic seizure, decompensating known glycogen storage disease (hepatic type)

pathophysiology_summary: In a hepatic glycogen storage disease the liver cannot mobilise glucose during fasting (impaired glycogenolysis/gluconeogenesis, classically glucose-6-phosphatase deficiency in GSD type I). The child is dependent on a continuous exogenous carbohydrate supply — overnight gastrostomy feeds and daytime uncooked cornstarch — to maintain euglycaemia. When the overnight feed disconnected and ~6 hours of fasting elapsed, blood glucose fell to a neuroglycopenic level (home meter 2.1 mmol/L), producing profound lethargy, an adrenergic response (pallor, diaphoresis, tachycardia) and a hypoglycaemic seizure. Because glucose-6-phosphate cannot be converted to free glucose, it is shunted to lactate, so fasting also drives a lactic acidosis — hence the compensatory tachypnoea. The seizure is SECONDARY to hypoglycaemia, not a primary epilepsy: correcting glucose is the treatment, not an anticonvulsant. The immediate priorities are rapid restoration of glucose (fast-acting carbohydrate) followed by sustained complex carbohydrate to prevent rebound, and emergency escalation because a remote assessor cannot secure the airway, confirm neurological recovery, or reverse acidosis.

differential.diagnosis: Fasting hypoglycaemia with hypoglycaemic seizure (metabolic decompensation)
differential.evidence_basis: A child dependent on continuous feeds who missed his overnight feed and then became unrousable and had a seizure — hypoglycaemia is the unifying mechanism.
A first seizure always warrants consideration, but here it is provoked and must not anchor management away from checking and correcting glucose (diagnostic overshadowing risk).
Intercurrent illness commonly precipitates metabolic crisis, so it must be screened for — but there is no fever, vomiting or diarrhoea here.
Infection is the other classic trigger of metabolic decompensation and can co-exist; afebrile with a clear mechanical precipitant makes it less likely but it cannot be excluded remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the leading line must be hypoglycaemic decompensation with an explicit plan to check/treat glucose; keeping epilepsy and sepsis on the list is good practice, anchoring solely on epilepsy is the trap.

differential.diagnosis: Fasting hypoglycaemia decompensating known hepatic GSD
differential.evidence_basis: Known feed-dependent metabolic condition + missed overnight feed + unrousable + seizure + pallor/sweating; classic fasting crisis.
No fever, vomiting or diarrhoea — the precipitant was purely mechanical (feed disconnection).
No prior seizures and not on anticonvulsants; a provoked (hypoglycaemic) seizure is far more likely than new primary epilepsy.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History alone should move the AI firmly to a hypoglycaemic mechanism and should prompt asking for a home glucose reading.

differential.diagnosis: Confirmed neuroglycopenia (home BGL 2.1 mmol/L) — hypoglycaemic seizure in hepatic GSD
differential.evidence_basis: A patient-obtained glucometer value of 2.1 mmol/L confirms profound hypoglycaemia as the seizure mechanism.
Deep fast breathing points to a compensated metabolic acidosis expected in GSD fasting; needs blood gas/lactate in hospital.
The documented profound hypoglycaemia fully explains the seizure without a primary neurological diagnosis.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The home BGL is patient-obtainable and admissible in telehealth (unverified); it should NOT lower urgency — a post-seizure neuroglycopenic child still needs emergency escalation.

differential.diagnosis: Acute fasting hypoglycaemia with hypoglycaemic seizure, decompensating hepatic glycogen storage disease, with probable fasting lactic acidosis
differential.evidence_basis: Missed feed → profound hypoglycaemia (2.1 mmol/L) → seizure and altered sensorium, with compensatory tachypnoea for lactic acidosis in a child who cannot fast.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is a time-critical metabolic emergency requiring immediate carbohydrate AND emergency transport; managing purely at home is not safe for a remote assessor.

red_flags.clinical_significance: Profound hypoglycaemia starves the brain and causes seizures; sustained neuroglycopenia risks irreversible neuronal injury and death.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Delayed glucose correction and failure to escalate can lead to recurrent seizures, coma and permanent brain injury.

red_flags.description: Profound documented hypoglycaemia (home BGL 2.1 mmol/L) after a missed feed
red_flags.clinical_significance: A value of 2.1 mmol/L is severe hypoglycaemia demanding immediate carbohydrate and confirms the mechanism of the seizure.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Not treating the glucose (e.g. treating the seizure as epilepsy) allows ongoing neuroglycopenia.

red_flags.description: High risk of recurrent hypoglycaemia over the next 12 hours in a feed-dependent child
red_flags.clinical_significance: Hepatic GSD gives essentially no fasting reserve; glucose can crash again quickly even after initial rescue.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: False reassurance from a transient improvement leads to a second, possibly worse, crisis.

red_flags.description: Fast, deep breathing suggesting compensated lactic acidosis
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals metabolic acidosis from fasting in GSD; requires blood gas/lactate and hospital correction.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-appreciated acidosis can worsen despite oral carbohydrate and needs IV management.

red_flags.description: Young child with an inborn error of metabolism and no fasting tolerance
red_flags.clinical_significance: Narrow physiologic reserve; any interruption of carbohydrate supply is potentially life-threatening and cannot be safely observed remotely.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Treating this as a routine unwell child under-triages a metabolic emergency.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Diagnostic overshadowing (attribute the seizure to epilepsy)
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the seizure as a new primary epilepsy in a child with a chronic condition and reaching for an anticonvulsant, instead of checking and correcting glucose. The seizure is a symptom of profound hypoglycaemia — glucose is the treatment.
Being reassured because the child perks up after glucose and standing down — a feed-dependent GSD child can crash again within an hour or two, and a remote assessor cannot confirm sustained recovery.

Attempting to fully manage a post-seizure neuroglycopenic child at home because the in-person note did so. The AI cannot measure serial glucose, secure the airway, or reverse acidosis — recognise, coach rescue carbohydrate, and escalate to 000.
Inventing or restating a gram/weight-based glucose or cornstarch dose for a child. Doses must come from the child's own metabolic emergency plan and the treating team — not be calculated remotely.

cannot_determine_via_chat: A verified laboratory or repeat capillary blood glucose
Blood gas, lactate and electrolytes to quantify the lactic acidosis
Blood pressure and an accurate heart rate
Palpation of the liver (degree of hepatomegaly)
Objective assessment of muscle tone, fasciculations and post-ictal neurological state
Whether the airway will remain protected if another seizure occurs
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 for an emergency ambulance now (post-seizure neuroglycopenic child), and while waiting coach the carer to give the child's OWN fast-acting glucose and reconnect the feed / give the usual cornstarch mixture per his established emergency plan; keep him safe and on his side if he seizes again.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low for the mechanism (hypoglycaemia is well supported); the real risks are UNDER-action (managing at home rather than escalating) and mistreatment (treating the seizure as epilepsy instead of correcting glucose).

guidelines_referenced.reference: BIMDG Emergency Management — Glycogen Storage Disease (hepatic)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: During fasting/illness, give fast-acting glucose to correct hypoglycaemia then sustained complex carbohydrate; escalate to hospital for IV glucose if not rapidly and durably corrected or if consciousness is impaired.
A hypoglycaemic seizure or impaired consciousness is a medical emergency — treat glucose immediately and arrange emergency assessment; do not withhold glucose to obtain confirmatory tests.
